NKJV.1: Now it happened on the second Sabbath after the first that He went through the grainfields. And His disciples plucked the heads of grain and ate them, rubbing them in their hands.
NKJV.2: And some of the Pharisees said to them, “Why are you doing what is not lawful to do on the Sabbath?”
NKJV.3: But Jesus answering them said, “Have you not even read this,  what David did when he was hungry, he and those who were with him:
NKJV.4: how he went into the house of God, took and ate the showbread, and also gave some to those with him,  which is not lawful for any but the priests to eat?”
NKJV.5: And He said to them, “The Son of Man is also Lord of the Sabbath.”
NKJV.6: Now it happened on another Sabbath, also, that He entered the synagogue and taught. And a man was there whose right hand was withered.
NKJV.7: So the scribes and Pharisees watched Him closely, whether He would heal on the Sabbath, that they might find an accusation against Him.
NKJV.8: But He knew their thoughts, and said to the man who had the withered hand, “Arise and stand here.” And he arose and stood.
NKJV.9: Then Jesus said to them, “I will ask you one thing:  Is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save life or to destroy?”
NKJV.10: And when He had looked around at them all, He said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” And he did so, and his hand was restored as whole as the other.
NKJV.11: But they were filled with rage, and discussed with one another what they might do to Jesus.
NKJV.12: Now it came to pass in those days that He went out to the mountain to pray, and continued all night in prayer to God.
NKJV.13: And when it was day, He called His disciples to Himself; and from them He chose twelve whom He also named apostles:
NKJV.14: Simon, whom He also named Peter, and Andrew his brother; James and John; Philip and Bartholomew;
NKJV.15: Matthew and Thomas; James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon called the Zealot;
NKJV.16: Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot who also became a traitor.
NKJV.17: And He came down with them and stood on a level place with a crowd of His disciples and a great multitude of people from all Judea and Jerusalem, and from the seacoast of Tyre and Sidon, who came to hear Him and be healed of their diseases,
NKJV.18: as well as those who were tormented with unclean spirits. And they were healed.
NKJV.19: And the whole multitude sought to touch Him, for power went out from Him and healed them all.
NKJV.20: Then He lifted up His eyes toward His disciples, and said: “Blessed are you poor, For yours is the kingdom of God.
NKJV.21: Blessed are you who hunger now, For you shall be filled. Blessed are you who weep now, For you shall laugh.
NKJV.22: Blessed are you when men hate you, And when they exclude you, And revile you, and cast out your name as evil, For the Son of Man’s sake.
NKJV.23: Rejoice in that day and leap for joy! For indeed your reward is great in heaven, For in like manner their fathers did to the prophets.
NKJV.24: “But  woe to you  who are rich, For  you have received your consolation.
NKJV.25: Woe to you who are full, For you shall hunger.  Woe to you who laugh now, For you shall mourn and  weep.
NKJV.26: Woe to you when all men speak well of you, For so did their fathers to the false prophets.
NKJV.27: “But I say to you who hear: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,
NKJV.28: bless those who curse you, and  pray for those who spitefully use you.
NKJV.29: To him who strikes you on the one cheek, offer the other also. And from him who takes away your cloak, do not withhold your tunic either.
NKJV.30: Give to everyone who asks of you. And from him who takes away your goods do not ask them back.
NKJV.31: And just as you want men to do to you, you also do to them likewise.
NKJV.32: “But if you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners love those who love them.
NKJV.33: And if you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners do the same.
NKJV.34: And if you lend to those from whom you hope to receive back, what credit is that to you? For even sinners lend to sinners to receive as much back.
NKJV.35: But  love your enemies,  do good, and  lend, hoping for nothing in return; and your reward will be great, and  you will be sons of the Most High. For He is kind to the unthankful and evil.
NKJV.36: Therefore be merciful, just as your Father also is merciful.
NKJV.37: “Judge not, and you shall not be judged. Condemn not, and you shall not be condemned.  Forgive, and you will be forgiven.
NKJV.38: Give, and it will be given to you: good measure, pressed down, shaken together, and running over will be put into your  bosom. For  with the same measure that you use, it will be measured back to you.”
NKJV.39: And He spoke a parable to them: “Can the blind lead the blind? Will they not both fall into the ditch?
NKJV.40: A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone who is perfectly trained will be like his teacher.
NKJV.41: And why do you look at the speck in your brother’s eye, but do not perceive the plank in your own eye?
NKJV.42: Or how can you say to your brother, ‘Brother, let me remove the speck that is in your eye,’ when you yourself do not see the plank that is in your own eye? Hypocrite! First remove the plank from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck that is in your brother’s eye.
NKJV.43: “For a good tree does not bear bad fruit, nor does a bad tree bear good fruit.
NKJV.44: For every tree is known by its own fruit. For men do not gather figs from thorns, nor do they gather grapes from a bramble bush.
NKJV.45: A good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings forth good; and an evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart brings forth evil. For out  of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaks.
NKJV.46: “But why do you call Me ‘Lord, Lord,’ and not do the things which I say?
NKJV.47: Whoever comes to Me, and hears My sayings and does them, I will show you whom he is like:
NKJV.48: He is like a man building a house, who dug deep and laid the foundation on the rock. And when the flood arose, the stream beat vehemently against that house, and could not shake it, for it was founded on the rock.
NKJV.49: But he who heard and did nothing is like a man who built a house on the earth without a foundation, against which the stream beat vehemently; and immediately it fell. And the ruin of that house was great.”

ESV.1: On a Sabbath,  while he was going through the grainfields, his disciples plucked and ate some heads of grain, rubbing them in their hands.
ESV.2: But some of the Pharisees said, “Why are you doing what is not lawful to do on the Sabbath?”
ESV.3: And Jesus answered them,  “Have you not read what David did when he was hungry, he and those who were with him:
ESV.4: how he entered the house of God and took and ate the bread of the Presence, which is not lawful for any but the priests to eat, and also gave it to those with him?”
ESV.5: And he said to them,  “The Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath.”
ESV.6: On another Sabbath, he entered the synagogue and was teaching, and a man was there whose right hand was withered.
ESV.7: And the scribes and the Pharisees watched him, to see whether he would heal on the Sabbath, so that they might find a reason to accuse him.
ESV.8: But he knew their thoughts, and he said to the man with the withered hand, “Come and stand here.” And he rose and stood there.
ESV.9: And Jesus said to them, “I ask you, is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do harm, to save life or to destroy it?”
ESV.10: And after looking around at them all he said to him, “Stretch out your hand.” And he did so, and his hand was restored.
ESV.11: But they were filled with fury and discussed with one another what they might do to Jesus.
ESV.12: In these days he went out to the mountain to pray, and all night he continued in prayer to God.
ESV.13: And when day came, he called his disciples and chose from them twelve, whom he named apostles:
ESV.14: Simon, whom he named Peter, and Andrew his brother, and James and John, and Philip, and Bartholomew,
ESV.15: and Matthew, and Thomas, and James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon who was called the Zealot,
ESV.16: and Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.
ESV.17: And he came down with them and stood on a level place, with a great crowd of his disciples and a great multitude of people from all Judea and Jerusalem and the seacoast of Tyre and Sidon,
ESV.18: who came to hear him and to be healed of their diseases. And those who were troubled with unclean spirits were cured.
ESV.19: And all the crowd sought to touch him, for power came out from him and healed them all.
ESV.20: And he lifted up his eyes on his disciples, and said: “Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.
ESV.21: “Blessed are you who are hungry now, for you shall be satisfied.  “Blessed are you who weep now, for you shall laugh.
ESV.22: “Blessed are you when people hate you and when they exclude you and revile you and spurn your name as evil, on account of the Son of Man!
ESV.23: Rejoice in that day, and leap for joy, for behold, your reward is great in heaven; for so their fathers did to the prophets.
ESV.24: “But woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation.
ESV.25: “Woe to you who are full now, for you shall be hungry. “Woe to you who laugh now, for you shall mourn and weep.
ESV.26: “Woe to you, when all people speak well of you, for so their fathers did to the false prophets.
ESV.27: “But I say to you who hear, Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,
ESV.28: bless those who curse you, pray for those who abuse you.
ESV.29: To one who strikes you on the cheek, offer the other also, and from one who takes away your cloak do not withhold your tunic  either.
ESV.30: Give to everyone who begs from you, and from one who takes away your goods do not demand them back.
ESV.31: And as you wish that others would do to you, do so to them.
ESV.32: “If you love those who love you, what benefit is that to you? For even sinners love those who love them.
ESV.33: And if you do good to those who do good to you, what benefit is that to you? For even sinners do the same.
ESV.34: And if you lend to those from whom you expect to receive, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, to get back the same amount.
ESV.35: But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing in return, and your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High, for he is kind to the ungrateful and the evil.
ESV.36: Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful.
ESV.37: “Judge not, and you will not be judged; condemn not, and you will not be condemned; forgive, and you will be forgiven;
ESV.38: give, and it will be given to you. Good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, will be put into your lap. For with the measure you use it will be measured back to you.”
ESV.39: He also told them a parable:  “Can a blind man lead a blind man? Will they not both fall into a pit?
ESV.40: A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone when he is fully trained will be like his teacher.
ESV.41: Why do you see the speck that is in your brother's eye, but do not notice the log that is in your own eye?
ESV.42: How can you say to your brother, ‘Brother, let me take out the speck that is in your eye,’ when you yourself do not see the log that is in your own eye? You hypocrite, first take the log out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take out the speck that is in your brother's eye.
ESV.43: “For no good tree bears bad fruit, nor again does a bad tree bear good fruit,
ESV.44: for each tree is known by its own fruit. For figs are not gathered from thornbushes, nor are grapes picked from a bramble bush.
ESV.45: The good person out of the good treasure of his heart produces good, and the evil person out of his evil treasure produces evil, for out of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaks.
ESV.46: “Why do you call me ‘Lord, Lord,’ and not do what I tell you?
ESV.47: Everyone who comes to me and hears my words and does them, I will show you what he is like:
ESV.48: he is like a man building a house, who dug deep and laid the foundation on the rock. And when a flood arose, the stream broke against that house and could not shake it, because it had been well built.
ESV.49: But the one who hears and does not do them is like a man who built a house on the ground without a foundation. When the stream broke against it, immediately it fell, and the ruin of that house was great.”

NASB.1: Now it happened that He was passing through [some] grainfields on a Sabbath; and His disciples were picking the heads of grain, rubbing them in their hands, and eating [the grain].
NASB.2: But some of the Pharisees said, 'Why do you do what is not lawful on the Sabbath?'
NASB.3: And Jesus answering them said, 'Have you not even read what David did when he was hungry, he and those who were with him,
NASB.4: how he entered the house of God, and took and ate the consecrated bread which is not lawful for any to eat except the priests alone, and gave it to his companions?'
NASB.5: And He was saying to them, 'The Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath.'
NASB.6: On another Sabbath He entered the synagogue and was teaching; and there was a man there whose right hand was withered.
NASB.7: The scribes and the Pharisees were watching Him closely [to see] if He healed on the Sabbath, so that they might find [reason] to accuse Him.
NASB.8: But He knew what they were thinking, and He said to the man with the withered hand, 'Get up and come forward!' And he got up and came forward.
NASB.9: And Jesus said to them, 'I ask you, is it lawful to do good or to do harm on the Sabbath, to save a life or to destroy it?'
NASB.10: After looking around at them all, He said to him, 'Stretch out your hand!' And he did [so]; and his hand was restored.
NASB.11: But they themselves were filled with rage, and discussed together what they might do to Jesus.
NASB.12: It was at this time that He went off to the mountain to pray, and He spent the whole night in prayer to God.
NASB.13: And when day came, He called His disciples to Him and chose twelve of them, whom He also named as apostles:
NASB.14: Simon, whom He also named Peter, and Andrew his brother; and James and John; and Philip and Bartholomew;
NASB.15: and Matthew and Thomas; James [the son] of Alphaeus, and Simon who was called the Zealot;
NASB.16: Judas [the son] of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.
NASB.17: Jesus came down with them and stood on a level place; and [there was] a large crowd of His disciples, and a great throng of people from all Judea and Jerusalem and the coastal region of Tyre and Sidon,
NASB.18: who had come to hear Him and to be healed of their diseases; and those who were troubled with unclean spirits were being cured.
NASB.19: And all the people were trying to touch Him, for power was coming from Him and healing [them] all.
NASB.20: And turning His gaze toward His disciples, He [began] to say, 'Blessed [are] you [who are] poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.
NASB.21: 'Blessed [are] you who hunger now, for you shall be satisfied. Blessed [are] you who weep now, for you shall laugh.
NASB.22: 'Blessed are you when men hate you, and ostracize you, and insult you, and scorn your name as evil, for the sake of the Son of Man.
NASB.23: 'Be glad in that day and leap [for joy], for behold, your reward is great in heaven. For in the same way their fathers used to treat the prophets.
NASB.24: 'But woe to you who are rich, for you are receiving your comfort in full.
NASB.25: 'Woe to you who are well-fed now, for you shall be hungry. Woe [to you] who laugh now, for you shall mourn and weep.
NASB.26: 'Woe [to you] when all men speak well of you, for their fathers used to treat the false prophets in the same way.
NASB.27: 'But I say to you who hear, love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,
NASB.28: bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you.
NASB.29: 'Whoever hits you on the cheek, offer him the other also; and whoever takes away your coat, do not withhold your shirt from him either.
NASB.30: 'Give to everyone who asks of you, and whoever takes away what is yours, do not demand it back.
NASB.31: 'Treat others the same way you want them to treat you.
NASB.32: 'If you love those who love you, what credit is [that] to you? For even sinners love those who love them.
NASB.33: 'If you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is [that] to you? For even sinners do the same.
NASB.34: 'If you lend to those from whom you expect to receive, what credit is [that] to you? Even sinners lend to sinners in order to receive back the same [amount].
NASB.35: 'But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing in return; and your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High; for He Himself is kind to ungrateful and evil [men].
NASB.36: 'Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.
NASB.37: 'Do not judge, and you will not be judged; and do not condemn, and you will not be condemned; pardon, and you will be pardoned.
NASB.38: 'Give, and it will be given to you. They will pour into your lap a good measure-- pressed down, shaken together, [and] running over. For by your standard of measure it will be measured to you in return.'
NASB.39: And He also spoke a parable to them: 'A blind man cannot guide a blind man, can he? Will they not both fall into a pit?
NASB.40: 'A pupil is not above his teacher; but everyone, after he has been fully trained, will be like his teacher.
NASB.41: 'Why do you look at the speck that is in your brother's eye, but do not notice the log that is in your own eye?
NASB.42: 'Or how can you say to your brother, 'Brother, let me take out the speck that is in your eye,' when you yourself do not see the log that is in your own eye? You hypocrite, first take the log out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take out the speck that is in your brother's eye.
NASB.43: 'For there is no good tree which produces bad fruit, nor, on the other hand, a bad tree which produces good fruit.
NASB.44: 'For each tree is known by its own fruit. For men do not gather figs from thorns, nor do they pick grapes from a briar bush.
NASB.45: 'The good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings forth what is good; and the evil [man] out of the evil [treasure] brings forth what is evil; for his mouth speaks from that which fills his heart.
NASB.46: 'Why do you call Me, 'Lord, Lord,' and do not do what I say?
NASB.47: 'Everyone who comes to Me and hears My words and acts on them, I will show you whom he is like:
NASB.48: he is like a man building a house, who dug deep and laid a foundation on the rock; and when a flood occurred, the torrent burst against that house and could not shake it, because it had been well built.
NASB.49: 'But the one who has heard and has not acted [accordingly], is like a man who built a house on the ground without any foundation; and the torrent burst against it and immediately it collapsed, and the ruin of that house was great.'

NLT.1: One Sabbath day as Jesus was walking through some grainfields, his disciples broke off heads of grain, rubbed off the husks in their hands, and ate the grain.
NLT.2: But some Pharisees said, “Why are you breaking the law by harvesting grain on the Sabbath?”
NLT.3: Jesus replied, “Haven’t you read in the Scriptures what David did when he and his companions were hungry?
NLT.4: He went into the house of God and broke the law by eating the sacred loaves of bread that only the priests can eat. He also gave some to his companions.”
NLT.5: And Jesus added, “The Son of Man is Lord, even over the Sabbath.”
NLT.6: On another Sabbath day, a man with a deformed right hand was in the synagogue while Jesus was teaching.
NLT.7: The teachers of religious law and the Pharisees watched Jesus closely. If he healed the man’s hand, they planned to accuse him of working on the Sabbath.
NLT.8: But Jesus knew their thoughts. He said to the man with the deformed hand, “Come and stand in front of everyone.” So the man came forward.
NLT.9: Then Jesus said to his critics, “I have a question for you. Does the law permit good deeds on the Sabbath, or is it a day for doing evil? Is this a day to save life or to destroy it?”
NLT.10: He looked around at them one by one and then said to the man, “Hold out your hand.” So the man held out his hand, and it was restored!
NLT.11: At this, the enemies of Jesus were wild with rage and began to discuss what to do with him.
NLT.12: One day soon afterward Jesus went up on a mountain to pray, and he prayed to God all night.
NLT.13: At daybreak he called together all of his disciples and chose twelve of them to be apostles. Here are their names:
NLT.15:
Matthew,
Thomas,
James (son of Alphaeus),
Simon (who was called the zealot),
NLT.16:
Judas (son of James),
Judas Iscariot (who later betrayed him).
NLT.17: When they came down from the mountain, the disciples stood with Jesus on a large, level area, surrounded by many of his followers and by the crowds. There were people from all over Judea and from Jerusalem and from as far north as the seacoasts of Tyre and Sidon.
NLT.18: They had come to hear him and to be healed of their diseases; and those troubled by evil spirits were healed.
NLT.19: Everyone tried to touch him, because healing power went out from him, and he healed everyone.
NLT.20:
Then Jesus turned to his disciples and said, “God blesses you who are poor,
for the Kingdom of God is yours.
NLT.21:
God blesses you who are hungry now,
for you will be satisfied.
God blesses you who weep now,
for in due time you will laugh.
NLT.22: What blessings await you when people hate you and exclude you and mock you and curse you as evil because you follow the Son of Man.
NLT.23: When that happens, be happy! Yes, leap for joy! For a great reward awaits you in heaven. And remember, their ancestors treated the ancient prophets that same way.
NLT.24:
“What sorrow awaits you who are rich,
for you have your only happiness now.
NLT.25:
What sorrow awaits you who are fat and prosperous now,
for a time of awful hunger awaits you.
What sorrow awaits you who laugh now,
for your laughing will turn to mourning and sorrow.
NLT.26:
What sorrow awaits you who are praised by the crowds,
for their ancestors also praised false prophets.
NLT.27: “But to you who are willing to listen, I say, love your enemies! Do good to those who hate you.
NLT.28: Bless those who curse you. Pray for those who hurt you.
NLT.29: If someone slaps you on one cheek, offer the other cheek also. If someone demands your coat, offer your shirt also.
NLT.30: Give to anyone who asks; and when things are taken away from you, don’t try to get them back.
NLT.31: Do to others as you would like them to do to you.
NLT.32: “If you love only those who love you, why should you get credit for that? Even sinners love those who love them!
NLT.33: And if you do good only to those who do good to you, why should you get credit? Even sinners do that much!
NLT.34: And if you lend money only to those who can repay you, why should you get credit? Even sinners will lend to other sinners for a full return.
NLT.35: “Love your enemies! Do good to them. Lend to them without expecting to be repaid. Then your reward from heaven will be very great, and you will truly be acting as children of the Most High, for he is kind to those who are unthankful and wicked.
NLT.36: You must be compassionate, just as your Father is compassionate.
NLT.37: “Do not judge others, and you will not be judged. Do not condemn others, or it will all come back against you. Forgive others, and you will be forgiven.
NLT.38: Give, and you will receive. Your gift will return to you in full — pressed down, shaken together to make room for more, running over, and poured into your lap. The amount you give will determine the amount you get back. ”
NLT.39: Then Jesus gave the following illustration: “Can one blind person lead another? Won’t they both fall into a ditch?
NLT.40: Students are not greater than their teacher. But the student who is fully trained will become like the teacher.
NLT.41: “And why worry about a speck in your friend’s eye when you have a log in your own?
NLT.42: How can you think of saying, ‘Friend, let me help you get rid of that speck in your eye,’ when you can’t see past the log in your own eye? Hypocrite! First get rid of the log in your own eye; then you will see well enough to deal with the speck in your friend’s eye.
NLT.43: “A good tree can’t produce bad fruit, and a bad tree can’t produce good fruit.
NLT.44: A tree is identified by its fruit. Figs are never gathered from thornbushes, and grapes are not picked from bramble bushes.
NLT.45: A good person produces good things from the treasury of a good heart, and an evil person produces evil things from the treasury of an evil heart. What you say flows from what is in your heart.
NLT.46: “So why do you keep calling me ‘Lord, Lord!’ when you don’t do what I say?
NLT.47: I will show you what it’s like when someone comes to me, listens to my teaching, and then follows it.
NLT.48: It is like a person building a house who digs deep and lays the foundation on solid rock. When the floodwaters rise and break against that house, it stands firm because it is well built.
NLT.49: But anyone who hears and doesn’t obey is like a person who builds a house right on the ground, without a foundation. When the floods sweep down against that house, it will collapse into a heap of ruins.”

WEB.1: Now on the second Sabbath after the first, he was going through the grain fields. His disciples plucked the heads of grain and ate, rubbing them in their hands.
WEB.2: But some of the Pharisees said to them, “Why do you do that which is not lawful to do on the Sabbath day?”
WEB.3: Jesus, answering them, said, “Haven’t you read what David did when he was hungry, he, and those who were with him;
WEB.4: how he entered into God’s house, and took and ate the show bread, and gave also to those who were with him, which is not lawful to eat except for the priests alone?”
WEB.5: He said to them, “The Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath.”
WEB.6: It also happened on another Sabbath that he entered into the synagogue and taught. There was a man there, and his right hand was withered.
WEB.7: The scribes and the Pharisees watched him, to see whether he would heal on the Sabbath, that they might find an accusation against him.
WEB.8: But he knew their thoughts; and he said to the man who had the withered hand, “Rise up, and stand in the middle.” He arose and stood.
WEB.9: Then Jesus said to them, “I will ask you something: Is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good, or to do harm? To save a life, or to kill?”
WEB.10: He looked around at them all, and said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” He did, and his hand was restored as sound as the other.

WEB.24: “But woe to you who are rich!  For you have received your consolation.

WEB.26: Woe,  when  men speak well of you,  for their fathers did the same thing to the false prophets.
WEB.27: “But I tell you who hear: love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,

WEB.31: “As you would like people to do to you, do exactly so to them.

WEB.33: If you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners do the same.
WEB.34: If you lend to those from whom you hope to receive, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, to receive back as much.
WEB.35: But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing back; and your reward will be great, and you will be children of the Most High; for he is kind toward the unthankful and evil.

WEB.37: Don’t judge,  and you won’t be judged.  Don’t condemn,  and you won’t be condemned.  Set free,  and you will be set free.

WEB.41: Why do you see the speck of chaff that is in your brother’s eye, but don’t consider the beam that is in your own eye?
WEB.42: Or how can you tell your brother, ‘Brother, let me remove the speck of chaff that is in your eye,’ when you yourself don’t see the beam that is in your own eye? You hypocrite! First remove the beam from your own eye, and then you can see clearly to remove the speck of chaff that is in your brother’s eye.
WEB.43: For there is no good tree that produces rotten fruit; nor again a rotten tree that produces good fruit.
WEB.44: For each tree is known by its own fruit. For people don’t gather figs from thorns, nor do they gather grapes from a bramble bush.
WEB.45: The good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings out that which is good, and the evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart brings out that which is evil, for out of the abundance of the heart, his mouth speaks.

WEB.48: He is like a man building a house, who dug and went deep, and laid a foundation on the rock. When a flood arose, the stream broke against that house, and could not shake it, because it was founded on the rock.
WEB.49: But he who hears, and doesn’t do, is like a man who built a house on the earth without a foundation, against which the stream broke, and immediately it fell, and the ruin of that house was great.”

AMP.1: One Sabbath while Jesus was passing through fields of standing grain, it happened that His disciples were picking the heads of grain, rubbing them in their hands, and eating them.
AMP.2: But some of the Pharisees said, "Why are you doing what is unlawful on the Sabbath?"
AMP.3: Jesus replied to them, "Have you not even read [in the Scriptures] what David did when he was hungry, he and those who were with him—
AMP.4: how he went into the house of God, and took and ate the consecrated bread, which is not lawful [for anyone] to eat except the priests alone, and [how he also] gave it to the men who were with him?"
AMP.5: Jesus was saying to them, "The Son of Man (the Messiah) is Lord [even] of the Sabbath."
AMP.6: On another Sabbath He went into the synagogue and taught, and a man was present whose right hand was withered.
AMP.7: The scribes and the Pharisees were watching Him closely [with malicious intent], to see if He would [actually] heal [someone] on the Sabbath, so that they might find a reason to accuse Him.
AMP.8: But He was aware of their thoughts, and He said to the man with the withered hand, "Get up and come forward." So he got up and stood there.
AMP.9: Then Jesus said to them, "I ask you directly: Is it lawful to do good on the Sabbath or to do evil, to save a life or to destroy it?"
AMP.10: After looking around at them all, He said to the man, "Stretch out your hand!" And he did, and his hand was [fully] restored.
AMP.11: But the scribes and Pharisees were filled with senseless rage [and lacked spiritual insight], and discussed with one another what they might do to Jesus.
AMP.12: Now at this time Jesus went off to the mountain to pray, and He spent the whole night in prayer to God.
AMP.13: When day came, He called His disciples and selected twelve of them, whom He also named apostles (special messengers, personally chosen representatives):
AMP.14: Simon, whom He also named Peter, and his brother Andrew; and [the brothers] James and John; and Philip, and Bartholomew [also called Nathanael];
AMP.15: and Matthew (Levi, the tax collector) and Thomas; and James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon who was called the Zealot;
AMP.16: Judas [also called Thaddaeus] the son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor [to the Lord].
AMP.17: Then Jesus came down with them and stood on a level place; and there was a large crowd of His disciples, and a vast multitude of people from all over Judea and Jerusalem and the coastal region of Tyre and Sidon,
AMP.18: who had come to listen to Him and to be healed of their diseases. Even those who were troubled by unclean spirits (demons) were being healed.
AMP.19: All the people were trying to touch Him, because [healing] power was coming from Him and healing them all.
AMP.20: And looking toward His disciples, He began speaking: "Blessed [spiritually prosperous, happy, to be admired] are you who are poor [in spirit, those devoid of spiritual arrogance, those who regard themselves as insignificant], for the kingdom of God is yours [both now and forever].
AMP.21: Blessed [joyful, nourished by God's goodness] are you who hunger now [for righteousness, actively seeking right standing with God], for you will be [completely] satisfied. Blessed [forgiven, refreshed by God's grace] are you who weep now [over your sins and repent], for you will laugh [when the burden of sin is lifted].
AMP.22: Blessed [morally courageous and spiritually alive with life-joy in God's goodness] are you when people hate you, and exclude you [from their fellowship], and insult you, and scorn your name as evil because of [your association with] the Son of Man.
AMP.23: Rejoice on that day and leap for joy, for your reward in heaven is great [absolutely inexhaustible]; for their fathers used to treat the prophets in the same way.
AMP.24: But woe (judgment is coming) to you who are rich [and place your faith in possessions while remaining spiritually impoverished], for you are [already] receiving your comfort in full [and there is nothing left to be awarded to you].
AMP.25: Woe to you who are well-fed (gorged, satiated) now, for you will be hungry. Woe to you who laugh now [enjoying a life of self-indulgence], for you will mourn and weep [and deeply long for God].
AMP.26: Woe to you when all the people speak well of you and praise you, for their fathers used to treat the false prophets in the same way.
AMP.27: "But I say to you who hear [Me and pay attention to My words]: Love [that is, unselfishly seek the best or higher good for] your enemies, [make it a practice to] do good to those who hate you,
AMP.28: bless and show kindness to those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you.
AMP.29: Whoever strikes you on the cheek, offer him the other one also [simply ignore insignificant insults or losses and do not bother to retaliate—maintain your dignity]. Whoever takes away your coat, do not withhold your shirt from him either.
AMP.30: Give to everyone who asks of you. Whoever takes away what is yours, do not demand it back.
AMP.31: Treat others the same way you want them to treat you.
AMP.32: If you [only] love those who love you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners love those who love them.
AMP.33: If you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners do the same.
AMP.34: If you lend [money] to those from whom you expect to receive [it back], what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners expecting to receive back the same amount.
AMP.35: But love [that is, unselfishly seek the best or higher good for] your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing in return; for your reward will be great (rich, abundant), and you will be sons of the Most High; because He Himself is kind and gracious and good to the ungrateful and the wicked.
AMP.36: Be merciful (responsive, compassionate, tender) just as your [heavenly] Father is merciful.
AMP.37: " Do not judge [others self-righteously], and you will not be judged; do not condemn [others when you are guilty and unrepentant], and you will not be condemned [for your hypocrisy]; pardon [others when they truly repent and change], and you will be pardoned [when you truly repent and change].
AMP.38: Give, and it will be given to you. They will pour into your lap a good measure—pressed down, shaken together, and running over [with no space left for more]. For with the standard of measurement you use [when you do good to others], it will be measured to you in return."
AMP.39: He also told them a parable: "Can a blind man guide [another] blind man? Will they not both fall into a hole in the ground?
AMP.40: A student is not superior to his teacher; but everyone, after he has been completely trained, will be like his teacher.
AMP.41: Why do you look at the speck that is in your brother's eye, but do not notice or consider the log that is in your own eye?
AMP.42: How can you say to your brother, 'Brother, allow me to take out the speck that is in your eye,' when you yourself do not see the log that is in your own eye? You hypocrite (play actor, pretender), first take the log out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take out the speck that is in your brother's eye.
AMP.43: For there is no good tree which produces bad fruit, nor, on the other hand, a bad tree which produces good fruit.
AMP.44: For each tree is known and identified by its own fruit. For figs are not picked from thorn bushes, nor is a cluster of grapes picked from a briar bush.
AMP.45: The [intrinsically] good man produces what is good and honorable and moral out of the good treasure [stored] in his heart; and the [intrinsically] evil man produces what is wicked and depraved out of the evil [in his heart]; for his mouth speaks from the overflow of his heart.
AMP.46: "Why do you call Me, 'Lord, Lord,' and do not practice what I tell you?
AMP.47: Everyone who comes to Me and listens to My words and obeys them, I will show you whom he is like:
AMP.48: he is like a [far-sighted, practical, and sensible] man building a house, who dug deep and laid a foundation on the rock; and when a flood occurred, the torrent burst against that house and yet could not shake it, because it had been securely built and founded on the rock.
AMP.49: But the one who has [merely] heard and has not practiced [what I say], is like a [foolish] man who built a house on the ground without any foundation, and the torrent burst against it; and it immediately collapsed, and the ruin of that house was great."

MSG.1: On a certain Sabbath Jesus was walking through a field of ripe grain. His disciples were pulling off heads of grain, rubbing them in their hands to get rid of the chaff, and eating them.
MSG.2: Some Pharisees said, "Why are you doing that, breaking a Sabbath rule?"
MSG.3: But Jesus stood up for them. "Have you never read what David and those with him did when they were hungry?
MSG.4: How he entered the sanctuary and ate fresh bread off the altar, bread that no one but priests were allowed to eat? He also handed it out to his companions."
MSG.5: Then he said, "The Son of Man is no slave to the Sabbath; he's in charge."
MSG.6: On another Sabbath he went to the meeting place and taught. There was a man there with a crippled right hand.
MSG.7: The religion scholars and Pharisees had their eye on Jesus to see if he would heal the man, hoping to catch him in a Sabbath infraction.
MSG.8: He knew what they were up to and spoke to the man with the crippled hand: "Get up and stand here before us." He did.
MSG.9: Then Jesus addressed them, "Let me ask you something: What kind of action suits the Sabbath best? Doing good or doing evil? Helping people or leaving them helpless?"
MSG.10: He looked around, looked each one in the eye. He said to the man, "Hold out your hand." He held it out—it was as good as new!
MSG.11: They were beside themselves with anger, and started plotting how they might get even with him.
MSG.12: At about that same time he climbed a mountain to pray. He was there all night in prayer before God.
MSG.13: The next day he summoned his disciples; from them he selected twelve he designated as apostles:
MSG.14: Simon, whom he named Peter, Andrew, his brother, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew,
MSG.15: Matthew, Thomas, James, son of Alphaeus, Simon, called the Zealot,
MSG.16: Judas, son of James, Judas Iscariot, who betrayed him.
MSG.17: Coming down off the mountain with them, he stood on a plain surrounded by disciples, and was soon joined by a huge congregation from all over Judea and Jerusalem, even from the seaside towns of Tyre and Sidon.
MSG.18: They had come both to hear him and to be cured of their ailments. Those disturbed by evil spirits were healed.
MSG.19: Everyone was trying to touch him—so much energy surging from him, so many people healed!
MSG.20: Then he spoke: You're blessed when you've lost it all. God's kingdom is there for the finding.
MSG.21: You're blessed when you're ravenously hungry. Then you're ready for the Messianic meal. You're blessed when the tears flow freely. Joy comes with the morning.
MSG.22: "Count yourself blessed every time someone cuts you down or throws you out, every time someone smears or blackens your name to discredit me. What it means is that the truth is too close for comfort and that that person is uncomfortable.
MSG.23: You can be glad when that happens—skip like a lamb, if you like!—for even though they don't like it, I do... and all heaven applauds. And know that you are in good company; my preachers and witnesses have always been treated like this.
MSG.24: But it's trouble ahead if you think you have it made. What you have is all you'll ever get.
MSG.25: And it's trouble ahead if you're satisfied with yourself. Your self will not satisfy you for long. And it's trouble ahead if you think life's all fun and games. There's suffering to be met, and you're going to meet it.
MSG.26: "There's trouble ahead when you live only for the approval of others, saying what flatters them, doing what indulges them. Popularity contests are not truth contests—look how many scoundrel preachers were approved by your ancestors! Your task is to be true, not popular.
MSG.27: "To you who are ready for the truth, I say this: Love your enemies. Let them bring out the best in you, not the worst.
MSG.28: When someone gives you a hard time, respond with the energies of prayer for that person.
MSG.29: If someone slaps you in the face, stand there and take it. If someone grabs your shirt, giftwrap your best coat and make a present of it.
MSG.30: If someone takes unfair advantage of you, use the occasion to practice the servant life. No more tit-for-tat stuff. Live generously.
MSG.31: "Here is a simple rule of thumb for behavior: Ask yourself what you want people to do for you; then grab the initiative and do it for them!
MSG.32: If you only love the lovable, do you expect a pat on the back? Run-of-the-mill sinners do that.
MSG.33: If you only help those who help you, do you expect a medal? Garden-variety sinners do that.
MSG.34: If you only give for what you hope to get out of it, do you think that's charity? The stingiest of pawnbrokers does that.
MSG.35: "I tell you, love your enemies. Help and give without expecting a return. You'll never—I promise—regret it. Live out this God-created identity the way our Father lives toward us, generously and graciously, even when we're at our worst.
MSG.36: Our Father is kind; you be kind.
MSG.37: "Don't pick on people, jump on their failures, criticize their faults—unless, of course, you want the same treatment. Don't condemn those who are down; that hardness can boomerang. Be easy on people; you'll find life a lot easier.
MSG.38: Give away your life; you'll find life given back, but not merely given back—given back with bonus and blessing. Giving, not getting, is the way. Generosity begets generosity."
MSG.39: He quoted a proverb: "'Can a blind man guide a blind man?' Wouldn't they both end up in the ditch?
MSG.40: An apprentice doesn't lecture the master. The point is to be careful who you follow as your teacher.
MSG.41: "It's easy to see a smudge on your neighbor's face and be oblivious to the ugly sneer on your own.
MSG.42: Do you have the nerve to say, 'Let me wash your face for you,' when your own face is distorted by contempt? It's this I-know-better-than-you mentality again, playing a holier-than-thou part instead of just living your own part. Wipe that ugly sneer off your own face and you might be fit to offer a washcloth to your neighbor.
MSG.43: "You don't get wormy apples off a healthy tree, nor good apples off a diseased tree.
MSG.44: The health of the apple tells the health of the tree. You must begin with your own life-giving lives.
MSG.45: It's who you are, not what you say and do, that counts. Your true being brims over into true words and deeds.
MSG.46: "Why are you so polite with me, always saying 'Yes, sir,' and 'That's right, sir,' but never doing a thing I tell you?
MSG.47: These words I speak to you are not mere additions to your life, homeowner improvements to your standard of living. They are foundation words, words to build a life on.
MSG.48: "If you work the words into your life, you are like a smart carpenter who dug deep and laid the foundation of his house on bedrock. When the river burst its banks and crashed against the house, nothing could shake it; it was built to last.
MSG.49: But if you just use my words in Bible studies and don't work them into your life, you are like a dumb carpenter who built a house but skipped the foundation. When the swollen river came crashing in, it collapsed like a house of cards. It was a total loss."

NIV.1:
Lord of the Sabbath
One Sabbath Jesus was going through the grainfields, and his disciples began to pick some heads of grain, rub them in their hands and eat the kernels.
NIV.2: Some of the Pharisees asked, “Why are you doing what is unlawful on the Sabbath?”
NIV.3: Jesus answered them, “Have you never read what David did when he and his companions were hungry?
NIV.4: He entered the house of God, and taking the consecrated bread, he ate what is lawful only for priests to eat. And he also gave some to his companions.”
NIV.5: Then Jesus said to them, “The Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath.”
NIV.6: On another Sabbath he went into the synagogue and was teaching, and a man was there whose right hand was shriveled.
NIV.7: The Pharisees and the teachers of the law were looking for a reason to accuse Jesus, so they watched him closely to see if he would heal on the Sabbath.
NIV.8: But Jesus knew what they were thinking and said to the man with the shriveled hand, “Get up and stand in front of everyone.” So he got up and stood there.
NIV.9: Then Jesus said to them, “I ask you, which is lawful on the Sabbath: to do good or to do evil, to save life or to destroy it?”
NIV.10: He looked around at them all, and then said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” He did so, and his hand was completely restored.
NIV.11: But they were furious and began to discuss with one another what they might do to Jesus.
NIV.12:
The Twelve Apostles
One of those days Jesus went out to a mountainside to pray, and spent the night praying to God.
NIV.13: When morning came, he called his disciples to him and chose twelve of them, whom he also designated apostles:
NIV.14: Simon (whom he named Peter), his brother Andrew, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew,
NIV.15: Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Simon who was called the Zealot,
NIV.16: Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.
NIV.17:
Blessings and Woes
He went down with them and stood on a level place. A large crowd of his disciples was there and a great number of people from all over Judea, from Jerusalem, and from the coast of Tyre and Sidon,
NIV.18: who had come to hear him and to be healed of their diseases. Those troubled by evil spirits were cured,
NIV.19: and the people all tried to touch him, because power was coming from him and healing them all.
NIV.20:
Looking at his disciples, he said:
“Blessed are you who are poor,
for yours is the kingdom of God.
NIV.21:
Blessed are you who hunger now,
for you will be satisfied.
Blessed are you who weep now,
for you will laugh.
NIV.22:
Blessed are you when men hate you,
when they exclude you and insult you
and reject your name as evil,
because of the Son of Man.
NIV.23: “Rejoice in that day and leap for joy, because great is your reward in heaven. For that is how their fathers treated the prophets.
NIV.24:
“But woe to you who are rich,
for you have already received your comfort.
NIV.25:
Woe to you who are well fed now,
for you will go hungry.
Woe to you who laugh now,
for you will mourn and weep.
NIV.26:
Woe to you when all men speak well of you,
for that is how their fathers treated the false prophets.
NIV.27:
Love for Enemies
“But I tell you who hear me: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,
NIV.28: bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you.
NIV.29: If someone strikes you on one cheek, turn to him the other also. If someone takes your cloak, do not stop him from taking your tunic.
NIV.30: Give to everyone who asks you, and if anyone takes what belongs to you, do not demand it back.
NIV.31: Do to others as you would have them do to you.
NIV.32: “If you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? Even ‘sinners’ love those who love them.
NIV.33: And if you do good to those who are good to you, what credit is that to you? Even ‘sinners’ do that.
NIV.34: And if you lend to those from whom you expect repayment, what credit is that to you? Even ‘sinners’ lend to ‘sinners,’ expecting to be repaid in full.
NIV.35: But love your enemies, do good to them, and lend to them without expecting to get anything back. Then your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High, because he is kind to the ungrateful and wicked.
NIV.36: Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.
NIV.37:
Judging Others
“Do not judge, and you will not be judged. Do not condemn, and you will not be condemned. Forgive, and you will be forgiven.
NIV.38: Give, and it will be given to you. A good measure, pressed down, shaken together and running over, will be poured into your lap. For with the measure you use, it will be measured to you.”
NIV.39: He also told them this parable: “Can a blind man lead a blind man? Will they not both fall into a pit?
NIV.40: A student is not above his teacher, but everyone who is fully trained will be like his teacher.
NIV.41: “Why do you look at the speck of sawdust in your brother’s eye and pay no attention to the plank in your own eye?
NIV.42: How can you say to your brother, ‘Brother, let me take the speck out of your eye,’ when you yourself fail to see the plank in your own eye? You hypocrite, first take the plank out of your eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother’s eye.
NIV.43:
A Tree and Its Fruit
“No good tree bears bad fruit, nor does a bad tree bear good fruit.
NIV.44: Each tree is recognized by its own fruit. People do not pick figs from thornbushes, or grapes from briers.
NIV.45: The good man brings good things out of the good stored up in his heart, and the evil man brings evil things out of the evil stored up in his heart. For out of the overflow of his heart his mouth speaks.
NIV.46:
The Wise and Foolish Builders
“Why do you call me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ and do not do what I say?
NIV.47: I will show you what he is like who comes to me and hears my words and puts them into practice.
NIV.48: He is like a man building a house, who dug down deep and laid the foundation on rock. When a flood came, the torrent struck that house but could not shake it, because it was well built.
NIV.49: But the one who hears my words and does not put them into practice is like a man who built a house on the ground without a foundation. The moment the torrent struck that house, it collapsed and its destruction was complete.”

NET.1: Jesus was going through the grain fields on a Sabbath, and his disciples picked some heads of wheat, rubbed them in their hands, and ate them.
NET.2: But some of the Pharisees said, “Why are you doing what is against the law on the Sabbath?”
NET.3: Jesus answered them, “Haven’t you read what David did when he and his companions were hungry —
NET.4: how he entered the house of God, took and ate the sacred bread, which is not lawful for any to eat but the priests alone, and gave it to his companions?”
NET.5: Then he said to them, “The Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath.”
NET.6: On another Sabbath, Jesus entered the synagogue and was teaching. Now a man was there whose right hand was withered.
NET.7: The experts in the law and the Pharisees watched Jesus closely to see if he would heal on the Sabbath, so that they could find a reason to accuse him.
NET.8: But he knew their thoughts, and said to the man who had the withered hand, “Get up and stand here.” So he rose and stood there.
NET.9: Then Jesus said to them, “I ask you, is it lawful to do good on the Sabbath or to do evil, to save a life or to destroy it?”
NET.10: After looking around at them all, he said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” The man did so, and his hand was restored.
NET.11: But they were filled with mindless rage and began debating with one another what they would do to Jesus.
NET.12: Now it was during this time that Jesus went out to the mountain to pray, and he spent all night in prayer to God.
NET.13: When morning came, he called his disciples and chose twelve of them, whom he also named apostles:
NET.14: Simon (whom he named Peter), and his brother Andrew; and James, John, Philip, Bartholomew,
NET.15: Matthew, Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, Simon who was called the Zealot,
NET.16: Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.
NET.17: Then he came down with them and stood on a level place. And a large number of his disciples had gathered along with a vast multitude from all over Judea, from Jerusalem, and from the seacoast of Tyre and Sidon. They came to hear him and to be healed of their diseases,
NET.18: and those who suffered from unclean spirits were cured.
NET.19: The whole crowd was trying to touch him, because power was coming out from him and healing them all.
NET.20: Then he looked up at his disciples and said: “Blessed are you who are poor, for the kingdom of God belongs to you.
NET.21: “Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. “Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh.
NET.22: “Blessed are you when people hate you, and when they exclude you and insult you and reject you as evil on account of the Son of Man!
NET.23: Rejoice in that day, and jump for joy, because your reward is great in heaven. For their ancestors did the same things to the prophets.
NET.24: “But woe to you who are rich, for you have received your comfort already.
NET.25: “Woe to you who are well satisfied with food now, for you will be hungry. “Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep.
NET.26: “Woe to you when all people speak well of you, for their ancestors did the same things to the false prophets.
NET.27: “But I say to you who are listening: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,
NET.28: bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you.
NET.29: To the person who strikes you on the cheek, offer the other as well, and from the person who takes away your coat, do not withhold your tunic either.
NET.30: Give to everyone who asks you, and do not ask for your possessions back from the person who takes them away.
NET.31: Treat others in the same way that you would want them to treat you.
NET.32: “If you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners love those who love them.
NET.33: And if you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? Even sinners do the same.
NET.34: And if you lend to those from whom you hope to be repaid, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, so that they may be repaid in full.
NET.35: But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing back. Then your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High, because he is kind to ungrateful and evil people.
NET.36: Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.
NET.37: “Do not judge, and you will not be judged; do not condemn, and you will not be condemned; forgive, and you will be forgiven.
NET.38: Give, and it will be given to you: A good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, will be poured into your lap. For the measure you use will be the measure you receive.”
NET.39: He also told them a parable: “Someone who is blind cannot lead another who is blind, can he? Won’t they both fall into a pit?

NET.41: Why do you see the speck in your brother’s eye, but fail to see the beam of wood in your own?
NET.42: How can you say to your brother, ‘Brother, let me remove the speck from your eye,’ while you yourself don’t see the beam in your own? You hypocrite! First remove the beam from your own eye, and then you can see clearly to remove the speck from your brother’s eye.
